items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: Which component handles visual and spatial information?
items.why: Students mix up the slave systems. The phonological loop is for auditory/verbal information; the sketchpad is for visual/spatial. The central executive coordinates but doesn't store.

items.q: Hallucinations and delusions are classified as which type of symptom?
items.why: Correct answer: Positive. “Negative” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Gottesman found MZ twin concordance for schizophrenia at:
items.why: Correct answer: 48%. “17%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the approximate lifetime risk (%) of schizophrenia in the general population?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
